{{short description|Capacity to mentally reconstruct personal events from the past}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=February 2024}}

In [[psychology]], '''mental time travel''' is the capacity to mentally reconstruct personal events from the past ([[episodic memory]]) as well as to imagine possible scenarios in the [[future]] (episodic [[Foresight (psychology)|foresight]]/episodic future thinking). The term was coined by Thomas Suddendorf and [[Michael Corballis]],<ref name="pmid9204544"/> building on 
[[Endel Tulving]]'s work on [[episodic memory]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/h0080017 |title=Memory and Consciousness |journal=Canadian Psychology |volume=26 |pages=1–12 |year=1985 | first = Endel | last = Tulving | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref> (Tulving proposed the alternative term '''chronesthesia'''.<ref>{{Cite book |doi=10.1093/acprof:oso/9780195134971.003.0020 |chapter=Chronesthesia: Conscious Awareness of Subjective Time |title=Principles of Frontal Lobe Function |pages=311–325 |year=2002 |last1=Tulving |first1=Endel | name-list-style = vanc |isbn=978-0-19-513497-1 }}</ref>)

Mental time travel has been studied by [[psychologists]], cognitive [[neuroscientists]], philosophers and in a variety of other academic disciplines.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.26613/esic.2.1.75 |title=The History and Future of Human Prospection |journal=Evolutionary Studies in Imaginative Culture |volume=2 |pages=75 |year=2018 | first = Adam | last = Bulley |s2cid=134294187 | name-list-style = vanc |url=http://psyarxiv.com/texvf/ |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Klein SB | title = The complex act of projecting oneself into the future | journal = Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Cognitive Science | volume = 4 | issue = 1 | pages = 63–79 | date = January 2013 | pmid = 26304175 | doi = 10.1002/wcs.1210 | url = https://philpapers.org/rec/KLETCA-2 | archive-date = 4 October 2018 | access-date = 4 October 2018 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20181004190021/https://philpapers.org/rec/KLETCA-2 | url-status = live }}</ref> Major areas of interest include the nature of the relationship between [[memory]] and foresight,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Suddendorf T | title = Episodic memory versus episodic foresight: Similarities and differences | journal = Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Cognitive Science | volume = 1 | issue = 1 | pages = 99–107 | date = January 2010 | pmid = 26272843 | doi = 10.1002/wcs.23 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Klein SB | title = Autonoetic consciousness: Reconsidering the role of episodic memory in future-oriented self-projection | journal = Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology | volume = 69 | issue = 2 | pages = 381–401 | year = 2018 | pmid = 25606713 | doi = 10.1080/17470218.2015.1007150 | s2cid = 14444260 | url = https://philpapers.org/rec/KLEAAR | archive-date = 4 October 2018 | access-date = 4 October 2018 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20181004185905/https://philpapers.org/rec/KLEAAR | url-status = live }}</ref> the [[evolution]] of the ability (including whether it is uniquely human or shared with other animals),<ref name=Suddendorf2013>{{cite book |last1=Suddendorf |first1=Thomas | name-list-style = vanc |title=The gap: the science of what separates us from other animals |publisher=Basic Books |year=2013 |isbn=978-0-465-03014-9 }}{{page needed|date=October 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cheke LG, Clayton NS | title = Mental time travel in animals | journal = Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Cognitive Science | volume = 1 | issue = 6 | pages = 915–930 | date = November 2010 | pmid = 26271786 | doi = 10.1002/wcs.59 }}</ref> its development in young children,<ref name=pmid23855564>{{cite journal | vauthors = Suddendorf T, Redshaw J | title = The development of mental scenario building and episodic foresight | journal = Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences | volume = 1296 | pages = 135–53 | date = August 2013 | issue = 1 | pmid = 23855564 | doi = 10.1111/nyas.12189 | bibcode = 2013NYASA1296..135S | s2cid = 33641357 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.lmot.2005.02.003 |title=The emergence of episodic future thinking in humans |journal=Learning and Motivation |volume=36 |issue=2 |pages=126–144 |year=2005 |last1=Atance |first1=Cristina M. |last2=o'Neill |first2=Daniela K. | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref> its underlying brain mechanisms,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Schacter DL, Addis DR, Hassabis D, Martin VC, Spreng RN, Szpunar KK | title = The future of memory: remembering, imagining, and the brain | journal = Neuron | volume = 76 | issue = 4 | pages = 677–94 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 23177955 | pmc = 3815616 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuron.2012.11.001 }}</ref><ref name=Irish2016>{{cite book |first1=Muireann |last1=Irish | name-list-style = vanc |chapter=Semantic Memory as the Essential Scaffold for Future-Oriented Mental Time Travel |pages=389–408 |chapter-url={{Google books|bkYnDAAAQBAJ|page=389|plainurl=yes}} |editor1-first=Kourken |editor1-last=Michaelian |editor2-first=Stanley B. |editor2-last=Klein |editor3-first=Karl K. |editor3-last=Szpunar |year=2016 |title=Seeing the Future: Theoretical Perspectives on Future-oriented Mental Time Travel |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-024153-7}}</ref> as well as its potential links to [[consciousness]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = D'Argembeau A, Van der Linden M | title = Predicting the phenomenology of episodic future thoughts | journal = Consciousness and Cognition | volume = 21 | issue = 3 | pages = 1198–206 | date = September 2012 | pmid = 22742997 | doi = 10.1016/j.concog.2012.05.004 | hdl = 2268/130764 | s2cid = 14972501 | url = https://orbi.uliege.be/handle/2268/130764 | archive-date = 7 June 2020 | access-date = 9 September 2020 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20200607173649/https://orbi.uliege.be/handle/2268/130764 | url-status = live }}</ref> the [[self]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = D'Argembeau A, Lardi C, Van der Linden M | title = Self-defining future projections: exploring the identity function of thinking about the future | journal = Memory | volume = 20 | issue = 2 | pages = 110–20 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22292616 | doi = 10.1080/09658211.2011.647697 | hdl = 2268/107158 | s2cid = 9399136 | hdl-access = free }}</ref> and [[free will]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Seligman ME, Railton P, Baumeister RF, Sripada C | title = Navigating into the Future or Driven by the Past | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 8 | issue = 2 | pages = 119–41 | date = March 2013 | pmid = 26172493 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612474317 | s2cid = 17506436 }}</ref>

==Overview, terminology, and relationship to other cognitive capacities==

[[Declarative memory]] refers to the capacity to store and retrieve information that can be explicitly expressed, and consists of both facts or knowledge about the world ([[semantic memory]]) and autobiographical details about one's own experiences ([[episodic memory]]).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Squire LR | title = Declarative and nondeclarative memory: multiple brain systems supporting learning and memory | journal = Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience | volume = 4 | issue = 3 | pages = 232–43 | year = 1992 | pmid = 23964880 | doi = 10.1162/jocn.1992.4.3.232 | s2cid = 15095727 }}</ref> Tulving (1985) originally suggested that episodic memory involved a kind of 'autonoetic' ('self-knowing') consciousness that required the first-person [[subjective experience]] of previously lived events, whereas [[semantic memory]] is associated with 'noetic' (knowing) consciousness but does not require such mental simulation.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tulving |first1=Endel | name-list-style = vanc |title=Elements of Episodic Memory |publisher=Oxford University |year=1985 |isbn=978-0-19-852125-9 }}{{page needed|date=October 2018}}</ref>

It has become increasingly clear that both semantic and episodic memory are integral for thinking about the future.<ref name=pmid17963565>{{cite journal | vauthors = Suddendorf T, Corballis MC | title = The evolution of foresight: What is mental time travel, and is it unique to humans? | journal = The Behavioral and Brain Sciences | volume = 30 | issue = 3 | pages = 299–313; discussion 313–51 | date = June 2007 | pmid = 17963565 | doi = 10.1017/S0140525X07001975 | url = https://espace.library.uq.edu.au/view/UQ:135952/Suddendorf-04122006.pdf | archive-date = 28 May 2015 | access-date = 12 December 2019 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20150528220122/http://espace.library.uq.edu.au/view/UQ:135952/Suddendorf-04122006.pdf | url-status = dead }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Irish M, Addis DR, Hodges JR, Piguet O | title = Considering the role of semantic memory in episodic future thinking: evidence from semantic dementia | journal = Brain | volume = 135 | issue = Pt 7 | pages = 2178–91 | date = July 2012 | pmid = 22614246 | doi = 10.1093/brain/aws119 | doi-access = free | hdl = 1959.4/unsworks_12991 | hdl-access = free }}</ref> Mental time travel, however, specifically refers to the 'autonoetic' systems, and thus selectively comprises episodic memory and episodic foresight.

The close link between episodic memory and episodic foresight has been established with evidence of their shared developmental trajectory,<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.lmot.2005.02.010 |title=Making decisions with the future in mind: Developmental and comparative identification of mental time travel |journal=Learning and Motivation |volume=36 |issue=2 |pages=110–125 |year=2005 |last1=Suddendorf |first1=Thomas |last2=Busby |first2=Janie | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref> similar impairment profiles in [[neuropsychiatric]] disease and in brain damage,<ref name = "Schacter_2007">{{cite journal | vauthors = Schacter DL, Addis DR | title = The cognitive neuroscience of constructive memory: remembering the past and imagining the future | journal = Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological Sciences | volume = 362 | issue = 1481 | pages = 773–86 | date = May 2007 | pmid = 17395575 | pmc = 2429996 | doi = 10.1098/rstb.2007.2087 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tulving E | title = Episodic memory: from mind to brain | journal = Annual Review of Psychology | volume = 53 | pages = 1–25 | year = 2002 | pmid = 11752477 | doi = 10.1146/annurev.psych.53.100901.135114 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1521/soco.20.5.353.21125 |title=Memory and Temporal Experience: The Effects of Episodic Memory Loss on an Amnesic Patient's Ability to Remember the Past and Imagine the Future |journal=Social Cognition |volume=20 |issue=5 |pages=353–379 |year=2002 |last1=Klein |first1=Stanley B. |last2=Loftus |first2=Judith |last3=Kihlstrom |first3=John F. |s2cid=19168428 | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref> phenomenological analyses,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = D'Argembeau A, Van der Linden M | title = Phenomenal characteristics associated with projecting oneself back into the past and forward into the future: influence of valence and temporal distance | journal = Consciousness and Cognition | volume = 13 | issue = 4 | pages = 844–58 | date = December 2004 | pmid = 15522635 | doi = 10.1016/j.concog.2004.07.007 | s2cid = 37248343 | url = https://orbi.uliege.be/bitstream/2268/2917/1/D%27Argembeau_ConCog_2004.pdf | type = Submitted manuscript | archive-date = 30 July 2021 | access-date = 28 August 2022 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20210730071808/https://orbi.uliege.be/bitstream/2268/2917/1/D%27Argembeau_ConCog_2004.pdf | url-status = live }}</ref> and with [[neuroimaging]].<ref name = "Okuda_2003">{{cite journal | vauthors = Okuda J, Fujii T, Ohtake H, Tsukiura T, Tanji K, Suzuki K, Kawashima R, Fukuda H, Itoh M, Yamadori A | title = Thinking of the future and past: the roles of the frontal pole and the medial temporal lobes | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 19 | issue = 4 | pages = 1369–80 | date = August 2003 | pmid = 12948695 | doi = 10.1016/S1053-8119(03)00179-4 | s2cid = 25549031 }}</ref> Mental time travel may be one of several processes enabled by a general scenario building or construction system in the brain.<ref name = "Schacter_2007" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cheng S, Werning M, Suddendorf T | title = Dissociating memory traces and scenario construction in mental time travel | journal = Neuroscience and Biobehavioral Reviews | volume = 60 | pages = 82–9 | date = January 2016 | pmid = 26627866 | doi = 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2015.11.011 | s2cid = 35479839 | url = https://espace.library.uq.edu.au/view/UQ:376207/UQ376207_OA.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hassabis D, Maguire EA | title = The construction system of the brain | journal = Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological Sciences | volume = 364 | issue = 1521 | pages = 1263–71 | date = May 2009 | pmid = 19528007 | pmc = 2666702 | doi = 10.1098/rstb.2008.0296 }}</ref> This general capacity to generate and reflect on mental scenarios has been compared to a theatre in the mind that depends on the working together of a host of components.<ref name=Irish2016/>
 
Investigations have been conducted into diverse aspects of mental time travel, including individual differences relating to [[personality]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Quoidbach J, Hansenne M, Mottet C | title = Personality and mental time travel: a differential approach to autonoetic consciousness | journal = Consciousness and Cognition | volume = 17 | issue = 4 | pages = 1082–92 | date = December 2008 | pmid = 18508283 | doi = 10.1016/j.concog.2008.04.002 | hdl = 2268/2368 | s2cid = 5654286 | url = http://orbi.ulg.ac.be/handle/2268/2368 | type = Submitted manuscript | hdl-access = free }}</ref> its instantiation in [[artificial intelligence]] systems,<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s11023-008-9095-5 |title=Coordinating with the Future: The Anticipatory Nature of Representation |journal=Minds and Machines |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=179–225 |year=2008 |last1=Pezzulo |first1=Giovanni | name-list-style = vanc |citeseerx=10.1.1.465.1691 |s2cid=3071800 }}</ref> and its relationship with [[theory of mind]]<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gaesser B, DiBiase HD, Kensinger EA | title = A role for affect in the link between episodic simulation and prosociality | journal = Memory | volume = 25 | issue = 8 | pages = 1052–1062 | date = September 2017 | pmid = 27841093 | doi = 10.1080/09658211.2016.1254246 | s2cid = 4765256 }}</ref> and [[mind-wandering]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Smallwood J, Schooler JW | title = The science of mind wandering: empirically navigating the stream of consciousness | journal = Annual Review of Psychology | volume = 66 | pages = 487–518 | date = January 2015 | pmid = 25293689 | doi = 10.1146/annurev-psych-010814-015331 | url = http://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/103321/1/2014.03.20_Annual_Review_Final_3.docx | doi-access = free | archive-date = 11 May 2022 | access-date = 9 September 2020 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220511220525/https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/103321/1/2014.03.20_Annual_Review_Final_3.docx | url-status = live }}</ref> The study of mental time travel in general terms is also related to – but distinct from – the study of the way individuals differ in terms of their [[future orientation]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Steinberg L, Graham S, O'Brien L, Woolard J, Cauffman E, Banich M | title = Age differences in future orientation and delay discounting | journal = Child Development | volume = 80 | issue = 1 | pages = 28–44 | year = 2009 | pmid = 19236391 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-8624.2008.01244.x | citeseerx = 10.1.1.537.1994 }}</ref> time perspective,<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0191-8869(97)00113-X |title=Present time perspective as a predictor of risky driving |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=23 |issue=6 |pages=1007–1023 |year=1997 |last1=Zimbardo |first1=Philip G. |last2=Keough |first2=Kelli A. |last3=Boyd |first3=John N. | name-list-style = vanc |doi-access=free }}</ref> and temporal self-continuity.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ersner-Hershfield H, Wimmer GE, Knutson B | title = Saving for the future self: neural measures of future self-continuity predict temporal discounting | journal = Social Cognitive and Affective Neuroscience | volume = 4 | issue = 1 | pages = 85–92 | date = March 2009 | pmid = 19047075 | pmc = 2656877 | doi = 10.1093/scan/nsn042 }}</ref>

=== Cultural and Linguistic Influences ===
While the neural substrates for mental time travel may be universal, the habitual deployment of this capacity is influenced by language and culture.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Pendleton |first=Jeffery R. L. |last2=Clayton |first2=Nicola S. |date=2026-01-21 |title=Time in mind: a multidisciplinary review on temporal perception, cognition, and memory |url=https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fcogn.2025.1688754/full |journal=Frontiers in Cognition |volume=4 |doi=10.3389/fcogn.2025.1688754 |doi-access=free|issn=2813-4532}}</ref> Linguistic patterns, such as the use of spatial metaphors (e.g., placing the future "behind" the body in Aymara) or grammatical structures like "future-time reference," can tune attention and bias how individuals represent and use time in decision-making.<ref name=":0" /> Furthermore, traditional event-based cultures demonstrate that mental time travel can operate over relative, situational chronologies rather than abstract, metric timelines.<ref name=":0" />

==Brain regions involved==
{{More citations needed section|date=April 2020}}Various [[neuroimaging]] studies have elucidated the [[brain]] systems underlying the capacity for mental time travel in adults. Early [[fMRI]] studies on the topic revealed a number of close correspondences between remembering past experiences and imagining future experiences in brain activity.<ref name = "Okuda_2003" />

===fMRI mapping of brain regions===
Addis et al. conducted an [[fMRI]] study to examine neural regions mediating construction and elaboration of past and future events.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Addis DR, Wong AT, Schacter DL | title = Remembering the past and imagining the future: common and distinct neural substrates during event construction and elaboration | journal = Neuropsychologia | volume = 45 | issue = 7 | pages = 1363–77 | date = April 2007 | pmid = 17126370 | pmc = 1894691 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuropsychologia.2006.10.016 }}</ref>

The elaboration phase, unlike the construction phase, has overlap in the cortical areas comprising the [[autobiographical memory]] retrieval network. In this study, it was also found that the left [[hippocampus]] and the right middle [[Occiput|occipital]] [[gyrus]] were significantly activated during past and future event construction, while the right hippocampus was significantly deactivated during past event construction. It was only activated during the creation of future events.{{citation needed|date=October 2018}} This differentiation suggests the right hippocampus may specifically respond to the novelty of constructing a new event representation <ref name=":0" /> . 

Episodic future thinking involves multiple component processes: retrieval and integration of relevant information from memory, processing of subjective time, and self-referential processing.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = D'Argembeau A, Stawarczyk D, Majerus S, Collette F, Van der Linden M, Feyers D, Maquet P, Salmon E | title = The neural basis of personal goal processing when envisioning future events | journal = Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience | volume = 22 | issue = 8 | pages = 1701–13 | date = August 2010 | pmid = 19642887 | doi = 10.1162/jocn.2009.21314 | s2cid = 32824508 | url = https://orbi.uliege.be/bitstream/2268/27700/1/D%27Argembeau%20et%20al_JOCN_2010.pdf | archive-date = 25 March 2024 | access-date = 1 December 2023 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20240325191437/https://orbi.uliege.be/bitstream/2268/27700/1/D%27Argembeau%20et%20al_JOCN_2010.pdf | url-status = live }}</ref> D'Argembeau et al.'s study found that the ventral [[medial prefrontal cortex]] and [[posterior cingulate]] cortex are the most activated areas when imagining future events that are relevant to one's personal goals than to unrelated ones. This shows that these brain regions play a role in personal goal processing, which is a critical feature of episodic future thinking.{{citation needed|date=October 2018}}

===Brain regions involved in the 'what' and 'where' of an event===
Cabeza et al. conducted a [[positron emission tomography]] (PET) scan study on a group of human test subjects to identify the brain regions involved in [[Time|temporal]] memory, which is based on a linear progression of events. Since 'recollecting a past episode involves remembering not only what happened but also when it happened', PET scans were used to find the areas of the brain that were activated when trying to remember a certain word in a sequence.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cabeza R, Mangels J, Nyberg L, Habib R, Houle S, McIntosh AR, Tulving E | title = Brain regions differentially involved in remembering what and when: a PET study | journal = Neuron | volume = 19 | issue = 4 | pages = 863–70 | date = October 1997 | pmid = 9354332 | doi = 10.1016/S0896-6273(00)80967-8 | s2cid = 924382 | doi-access = free }}</ref> The results show that temporal-order memory of past events involves the frontal and posterior brain regions and item retrieval shows neural activity in the medial temporal and basal fore brain regions.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cabeza|first1=Roberto|last2=Mangels|first2=Jennifer|last3=Nyberg|first3=Lars|last4=Habib|first4=Reza|last5=Houle|first5=Sylvain|last6=McIntosh|first6=Anthony R|last7=Tulving|first7=Endel|date=1997-10-01|title=Brain Regions Differentially Involved in Remembering What and When: a PET Study|journal=Neuron|language=en|volume=19|issue=4|pages=863–870|doi=10.1016/S0896-6273(00)80967-8|issn=0896-6273|pmid=9354332|s2cid=924382|doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Evolution and human uniqueness==

The ability to travel mentally in time – especially into the future – has been highlighted as a potential prime mover in [[human evolution]], enabling humans to prepare, plan and shape the future to their advantage.<ref name=Suddendorf2013/> However, the question of whether or to what extent animals other than human beings can engage in mental time travel has remained controversial.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Roberts WA | title = Are animals stuck in time? | journal = Psychological Bulletin | volume = 128 | issue = 3 | pages = 473–89 | date = May 2002 | pmid = 12002698 | doi = 10.1037/0033-2909.128.3.473 | s2cid = 7729195 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |first1=Jonathan |last1=Redshaw |first2=Adam |last2=Bulley | name-list-style = vanc |chapter=Future-Thinking in Animals: Capacities and Limits |pages=31–51 |chapter-url={{Google books|srk8DwAAQBAJ|page=31|plainurl=yes}} |editor1-first=Gabriele |editor1-last=Oettingen |editor2-first=A. Timur |editor2-last=Sevincer |editor3-first=Peter M. |editor3-last=Gollwitzer |year=2018 |title=The Psychology of Thinking about the Future |publisher=Guilford Publications |isbn=978-1-4625-3441-8 }}</ref>

One proposal, the Bischof-Köhler hypothesis,<ref name=pmid9204544>{{cite journal | vauthors = Suddendorf T, Corballis MC | title = Mental time travel and the evolution of the human mind | journal = Genetic, Social, and General Psychology Monographs | volume = 123 | issue = 2 | pages = 133–67 | date = May 1997 | pmid = 9204544 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Bischof |first1=Norbert |name-list-style=vanc |title=Das Rätsel Ödipus |trans-title=The riddle of Oedipus |language=de |publisher=Piper |year=1985 |isbn=978-3-492-02962-9 |url=https://epub.ub.uni-muenchen.de/2850/ |archive-date=4 October 2018 |access-date=4 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181004231613/https://epub.ub.uni-muenchen.de/2850/ |url-status=live }}{{page needed|date=October 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Bischof-Köhler |first1=Doris |name-list-style=vanc |year=1985 |chapter=Zur Phylogenese menschlicher Motivation |chapter-url=https://epub.ub.uni-muenchen.de/2921/ |trans-chapter=On the phylogenesis of human motivation |editor1-last=Eckensberger |editor1-first=Lutz H. |editor2-last=Baltes |editor2-first=Margret M. |title=Emotion und Reflexivität |trans-title=Emotion and reflexivity |language=de |location=München |publisher=Urban & Schwarzenberg |pages=3–47 |isbn=978-3-541-14251-4 |archive-date=4 October 2018 |access-date=4 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181004230416/https://epub.ub.uni-muenchen.de/2921/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Köhler W |title=The mentality of apes |publisher=Read Books |year=2013 }}{{page needed|date=October 2018}}</ref> posits that non-human animals cannot act upon drive states they do not currently possess, for example seeking out water while currently fully quenched. Other proposals suggest that different species may have some capacities, but are limited because of shortcomings in a range of component capacities of mental scenario building and imagination.<ref name=pmid17963565/> A number of studies have claimed to have demonstrated mental time travel in animals including, most notably, various [[great apes]], crows, ravens, and [[western scrub jay]]s,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kabadayi C, Osvath M | title = Ravens parallel great apes in flexible planning for tool-use and bartering | journal = Science | volume = 357 | issue = 6347 | pages = 202–204 | date = July 2017 | pmid = 28706072 | doi = 10.1126/science.aam8138 | bibcode = 2017Sci...357..202K | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mulcahy NJ, Call J | title = Apes save tools for future use | journal = Science | volume = 312 | issue = 5776 | pages = 1038–40 | date = May 2006 | pmid = 16709782 | doi = 10.1126/science.1125456 | bibcode = 2006Sci...312.1038M | s2cid = 8468216 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Osvath M, Osvath H | title = Chimpanzee (Pan troglodytes) and orangutan (Pongo abelii) forethought: self-control and pre-experience in the face of future tool use | journal = Animal Cognition | volume = 11 | issue = 4 | pages = 661–74 | date = October 2008 | pmid = 18553113 | doi = 10.1007/s10071-008-0157-0 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.183.6296 | s2cid = 207050786 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Raby CR, Alexis DM, Dickinson A, Clayton NS | title = Planning for the future by western scrub-jays | journal = Nature | volume = 445 | issue = 7130 | pages = 919–21 | date = February 2007 | pmid = 17314979 | doi = 10.1038/nature05575 | bibcode = 2007Natur.445..919R | s2cid = 4405897 }}</ref> but these have been subjected to a number of criticisms and simpler alternative explanations have been proposed for the results.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Suddendorf T, Corballis MC, Collier-Baker E | title = How great is great ape foresight? | journal = Animal Cognition | volume = 12 | issue = 5 | pages = 751–4 | date = September 2009 | pmid = 19565281 | doi = 10.1007/s10071-009-0253-9 | s2cid = 7364241 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Suddendorf T | title = Behavior. Foresight and evolution of the human mind | journal = Science | volume = 312 | issue = 5776 | pages = 1006–7 | date = May 2006 | pmid = 16709773 | doi = 10.1126/science.1129217 | jstor = 3846137 | s2cid = 29830044 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Redshaw J, Taylor AH, Suddendorf T | title = Flexible Planning in Ravens? | journal = Trends in Cognitive Sciences | volume = 21 | issue = 11 | pages = 821–822 | date = November 2017 | pmid = 28927634 | doi = 10.1016/j.tics.2017.09.001 | s2cid = 20496597 | url = https://espace.library.uq.edu.au/view/UQ:685380/UQ685380_OA.pdf }}</ref> This debate is ongoing.<ref name=Suddendorf2013/>

If mental time travel is unique to humans, then it must have emerged over the last 6 million years since the line leading to modern humans split from the line leading to modern [[Common chimpanzee|chimpanzees]]. Perhaps the first hard evidence for the evolution of mental time travel in humans comes in the form of [[Acheulean]] [[Biface|bifacial handaxes]] associated with [[Homo erectus]]. Acheulean tools are complex and appear to have required advanced planning to create.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wynn T, Coolidge FL | title = Archeological insights into hominin cognitive evolution | journal = Evolutionary Anthropology | volume = 25 | issue = 4 | pages = 200–13 | date = July 2016 | pmid = 27519459 | doi = 10.1002/evan.21496 | s2cid = 12334658 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1086/650296 |title=Coevolution of Composite-Tool Technology, Constructive Memory, and Language |journal=Current Anthropology |volume=51 |pages=S135–S147 |year=2010 |last1=Ambrose |first1=Stanley H. |s2cid=142657600 | name-list-style = vanc}}</ref> There is also evidence that they were often crafted in one location and then taken elsewhere for repeated use.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hallos J | title = "15 Minutes of Fame": exploring the temporal dimension of middle pleistocene lithic technology | journal = Journal of Human Evolution | volume = 49 | issue = 2 | pages = 155–79 | date = August 2005 | pmid = 15964609 | doi = 10.1016/j.jhevol.2005.03.002 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.628.4393 }}</ref>

A number of important adaptive functions have been identified that rely to some degree on the capacity to remember the past and imagine the future.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Schacter DL, Benoit RG, Szpunar KK | title = Episodic Future Thinking: Mechanisms and Functions | journal = Current Opinion in Behavioral Sciences | volume = 17 | pages = 41–50 | date = October 2017 | pmid = 29130061 | pmc = 5675579 | doi = 10.1016/j.cobeha.2017.06.002 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.cobeha.2018.01.019 |title=Prospection and natural selection |journal=Current Opinion in Behavioral Sciences |volume=24 |pages=26–31 |year=2018 | vauthors = Suddendorf T, Bulley A, Miloyan B |s2cid=53180176 }}</ref> These functions include predicting future emotional reactions ([[affective forecasting]]),<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gilbert DT, Wilson TD | title = Prospection: experiencing the future | journal = Science | volume = 317 | issue = 5843 | pages = 1351–4 | date = September 2007 | pmid = 17823345 | doi = 10.1126/science.1144161 | bibcode = 2007Sci...317.1351G | s2cid = 19753427 }}</ref> deliberate practice,<ref>{{cite book |last1=Suddendorf |first1=Thomas |last2=Brinums |first2=Melissa |last3=Imuta |first3=Kana | name-list-style = vanc |year=2015 |chapter=Shaping one's future self: the development of deliberate practice |pages=343–66 |chapter-url={{Google books|iL6SDAAAQBAJ|page=343|plainurl=yes}} |editor1-first=Kourken |editor1-last=Michaelian |editor2-first=Stanley B. |editor2-last=Klein |editor3-first=Karl K. |editor3-last=Szpunar |title=Seeing the future: theoretical perspectives on future-oriented mental time travel |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-024154-4}}</ref> [[intertemporal choice]],<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/gpr0000061 |title=Prospection and the present moment: The role of episodic foresight in intertemporal choices between immediate and delayed rewards |journal=Review of General Psychology |volume=20 |pages=29–47 |year=2016 |last1=Bulley |first1=Adam |last2=Henry |first2=Julie |last3=Suddendorf |first3=Thomas |s2cid=84178870 | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref> [[navigation]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Arnold AE, Iaria G, Ekstrom AD | title = Mental simulation of routes during navigation involves adaptive temporal compression | journal = Cognition | volume = 157 | pages = 14–23 | date = December 2016 | pmid = 27568586 | pmc = 5143185 | doi = 10.1016/j.cognition.2016.08.009 }}</ref> [[prospective memory]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Terrett G, Rose NS, Henry JD, Bailey PE, Altgassen M, Phillips LH, Kliegel M, Rendell PG | title = The relationship between prospective memory and episodic future thinking in younger and older adulthood | journal = Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology | volume = 69 | issue = 2 | pages = 310–23 | year = 2018 | pmid = 26018341 | doi = 10.1080/17470218.2015.1054294 | s2cid = 18755775 }}</ref> [[counterfactual thinking]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Schacter DL, Benoit RG, De Brigard F, Szpunar KK | title = Episodic future thinking and episodic counterfactual thinking: intersections between memory and decisions | journal = Neurobiology of Learning and Memory | volume = 117 | pages = 14–21 | date = January 2015 | pmid = 24373942 | pmc = 4071128 | doi = 10.1016/j.nlm.2013.12.008 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Byrne RM | title = Mental models and counterfactual thoughts about what might have been | journal = Trends in Cognitive Sciences | volume = 6 | issue = 10 | pages = 426–431 | date = October 2002 | pmid = 12413576 | doi = 10.1016/S1364-6613(02)01974-5 | s2cid = 18582118 }}</ref> and [[planning]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Klein SB, Robertson TE, Delton AW | title = Facing the future: memory as an evolved system for planning future acts | journal = Memory & Cognition | volume = 38 | issue = 1 | pages = 13–22 | date = January 2010 | pmid = 19966234 | pmc = 3553218 | doi = 10.3758/MC.38.1.13 }}</ref>

Recent theoretical frameworks, introduced by Jeffery R. L. Pendleton and Prof. [[Nicky Clayton|Nicola S. Clayton]] at the [[University of Cambridge]], distinguish between objective physical time and "mind time," defined as the subjective, agentic navigation of a dynamic temporal landscape.<ref name=":0" /> While individual mental time travel (MTT) exists in other species, humans are thought to uniquely engage in transpersonal extended mental time travel (teMTT).<ref name=":0" /> This capacity couples individual episodic simulation with shared narratives, storytelling, and external artifacts (such as clocks and calendars) to synchronize mind time across individuals.<ref name=":0" /> This system enables the vicarious experience of remote histories and the collective coordination of futures across generations.<ref name=":0" />

===Episodic-like memory and planning for the future in great apes===
Osvath et al. conducted a study on apes to show that they have the ability of foresight. The study consisted of testing for self-control, [[associative learning]], and envisioning in chimpanzees and orangutans through a series of experiments.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Osvath M | title = Great ape foresight is looking great | journal = Animal Cognition | volume = 13 | issue = 5 | pages = 777–81 | date = September 2010 | pmid = 20607575 | doi = 10.1007/s10071-010-0336-7 | s2cid = 6931050 }}</ref> Critics questioned whether these animals truly exhibited mental time travel, or whether it was associative learning that caused them to behave as they did. The Bischof-Kohler hypothesis says that animals cannot anticipate future needs, and this study by Osvath tried to disprove the hypothesis.{{citation needed|date=October 2018}}

The scientists showed that when the apes were presented with a food item in conjunction with a utensil that could be used to actually eat that particular food, these animals chose the utensil instead of food. They anticipated a future need for the utensil that overcame the current want for just a food reward. This is an example of mental time travel in animals. It was not a result of associative learning, that they actually chose the utensil instead of the food reward, since the scientists ran another experiment to account for that. Other examples, such as food caching by birds, may be examples of mental time travel in non-humans. Even survival instinct by certain animals such as elephants, in response to imminent danger, could involve mental time travel mechanisms.{{citation needed|date=October 2018}}

Another study to show that great apes have the ability of foresight was conducted by Martin-Ordas G. et al. These scientists were able to show that "apes remember in an integrated fashion what, where and when" a particular event had happened.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Martin-Ordas G, Haun D, Colmenares F, Call J | title = Keeping track of time: evidence for episodic-like memory in great apes | journal = Animal Cognition | volume = 13 | issue = 2 | pages = 331–40 | date = March 2010 | pmid = 19784852 | pmc = 2822233 | doi = 10.1007/s10071-009-0282-4 }}</ref> Two experiments were conducted in this study, the first being an investigation of the content of the memories of apes i.e. could these animals remember when and where two types of food they were shown before are hidden. The second experiment explored the structure of the memories. It was found that the apes' memories were formed in an integrated what–where–when structure. All these findings suggest that it is not instinctive or learning predispositions that made the animals behave the way they did, but rather that they have the ability to mentally time travel. However, comparative psychologists are divided about this conclusion.<ref name=Suddendorf2013/>

===Episodic-like memory in western scrub-jays===
In their study to show that birds exhibit [[episodic-like memory]], Clayton et al. used 3 behavioral criteria: content, structure, and flexibility, to decide whether the food caching habits of these birds were evidence of their ability to recall the past and plan for the future.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Clayton NS, Bussey TJ, Dickinson A | title = Can animals recall the past and plan for the future? | journal = Nature Reviews. Neuroscience | volume = 4 | issue = 8 | pages = 685–91 | date = August 2003 | pmid = 12894243 | doi = 10.1038/nrn1180 | s2cid = 11064341 }}</ref> Content involved remembering what happened based on a specific past experience. Structure required the formation of a 'what-where-when' representation of the event. Finally, flexibility was used to see how well the information could be organized and re-organized, based on facts and experiences. Mental time travel involves the use of both episodic future thinking and semantic knowledge. This study also contradicts the Bischof-Kohler hypothesis by showing that some animals may mentally time travel into the future or back to the past. However this interpretation has remained controversial.<ref name=Suddendorf2013/>

=== Episodic-like memory in cuttlefish ===
Beyond vertebrates, recent research on cephalopods suggests that mental time travel may have evolved convergently in species with vastly different neural architectures. Cuttlefish (''Sepia officinalis'') have demonstrated episodic-like memory by retrieving "what-where-when" information and showing graded self-control in delay-of-gratification tasks.<ref name=":0" /> This suggests that the capacity to integrate past events to guide future behavior is an adaptive response to ecological pressures like solitary foraging, rather than a trait exclusive to social mammals or birds.<ref name=":0" />

==Development in children==
Studies into the development of mental time travel in [[infancy]] suggest that the involved component processes come online piece by piece. Most of the required psychological subcomponents appear to be available by approximately age four.<ref name=pmid23855564/> This includes the fundamental capacity to prepare for two [[mutually exclusive]] possible future events, which appears to develop between the ages of 3 and 5.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Redshaw J, Suddendorf T | title = Children's and Apes' Preparatory Responses to Two Mutually Exclusive Possibilities | journal = Current Biology | volume = 26 | issue = 13 | pages = 1758–1762 | date = July 2016 | pmid = 27345164 | doi = 10.1016/j.cub.2016.04.062 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Two and three-year-old children can report some information about upcoming events, and by ages four and five, children can talk more clearly about future situations.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1353/mpq.2006.0005 |title=The Development of Future Time Concepts Through Mother-Child Conversation |journal=Merrill-Palmer Quarterly |volume=52 |pages=70–95 |year=2006 |last1=Hudson |first1=Judith A. |s2cid=144754946 | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref> However, there is concern that children may understand more than they can articulate, and that they may say things without fully understanding. Thus, researchers have tried to examine future-oriented action.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/cdep.12128 |title=Young Children's Thinking About the Future |journal=Child Development Perspectives |volume=9 |issue=3 |pages=178–182 |year=2015 |last1=Atance |first1=Cristina M. | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref> A carefully controlled study found that four-year-olds could already remember a specific problem they saw in a different room sufficiently enough to prepare for its future solution.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Suddendorf T, Nielsen M, von Gehlen R | title = Children's capacity to remember a novel problem and to secure its future solution | journal = Developmental Science | volume = 14 | issue = 1 | pages = 26–33 | date = January 2011 | pmid = 21159085 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-7687.2010.00950.x }}</ref> These results suggest that children by the end of the [[preschool]] years have developed some fundamental capacity for foresight, capacities that continue to develop throughout [[childhood]] and [[adolescence]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/mbe.12124 |title=The Neurocognitive Development of Episodic Prospection and its Implications for Academic Achievement |journal=Mind, Brain, and Education |volume=10 |issue=3 |pages=196–206 |year=2016 |last1=Prabhakar |first1=Janani |last2=Coughlin |first2=Christine |last3=Ghetti |first3=Simona | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref>

==Measurement==
Studies of mental time travel require the [[measurement]] of both [[episodic memory]] and episodic foresight.

===Episodic memory===
Episodic memory is typically measured in human adults by asking people to report or describe past events that they had experienced. Many studies provide participants with information at one point in the study and then assess their memory for this information at a later point in the study. The advantage of these studies is that they allow the accuracy of recall to be assessed. However, Cheke and Clayton found that such different measurements of memory do not mutually correspond enough and may therefore capture different facets of memory.<ref name=pmid23955172>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cheke LG, Clayton NS | title = Do different tests of episodic memory produce consistent results in human adults? | journal = Learning and Memory | volume = 20 | issue = 9 | pages = 491–8 | date = Aug 2013 | pmid = 23955172 | doi=10.1101/lm.030502.113| doi-access = free }}</ref> A more general limitation with studies in this field is that they fail to capture people's actual memories for real-life events, just like in other studied animals.

Many studies focus on asking people to recall episodes from their own lives. Some of them attempt to verify the accuracy of recall by comparing participants' memories to those of family, friends or relatives who experienced the same event,<ref name=pmid16448975>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tranel D, Jones RD | title = Knowing "what" knowing "when" | journal = Journal of Clinical and Experimental Neuropsychology | volume = 28 | issue = 1 | pages = 43–66 | date = Jan 2006 | pmid = 16448975 | doi=10.1080/13803390490919344| s2cid = 23283480 }}</ref> or in some cases by comparing peoples' memories of an event to public information about the event.<ref name=pmid19397377>{{cite journal |vauthors=Hirst W, Phelphs EA, Buckner RL, Budson AE, Cuc A, Gabrieli JD, Johnson MK, Lyle KB, Lustig C, Mather M, Meksin R, Mitchell KJ, Ochsner KN, Schacter DL, Simons JS, Vaidya CJ | title = Long-term memory for the terrorist attack of 11 September: Flashbulb memories, event memories, and the factors that influence their retention | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology: General | volume = 138 | issue = 2 | pages = 161–76 | date = May 2009 | pmid = 19397377 | doi=10.1037/a0015527| pmc = 2925254 }}</ref> However it is not always easy to verify the accuracy of recall, so many measures of episodic memory do not do this, focusing instead on aspects of people's verbal descriptions of their memories.

Three commonly used measures that do not verify the accuracy of people's memories are as follows:
#Dritschel et al. adapted the [[Controlled Oral Word Association Test]] to assess the fluency with which people recall personal autobiographical episodes in specific given time periods (e.g., last week, last year, last 5 years, etc.) in a specific time limit (e.g., 1 min).<ref name=pmid1565011>{{cite journal |vauthors=Dritschel BH, Williams JM, Baddeley AD, Nimmo-Smith I | title = Autobiographical fluency: A method for the study of personal memory | journal = Memory and Cognition | volume = 20 | issue = 2 | pages = 133–40 | date = Mar 1992 | pmid = 1565011 | doi=10.3758/bf03197162| doi-access = free }}</ref>
#	Baddeley and Wilson used a 4-point scale with which to rate participants' memories as (3) specific, (2) intermediate, (1) general, and (0) nil, based on the level of the detail provided in their description.<ref>{{cite book |vauthors=Baddeley A, Wilson B | chapter = Amnesia, autobiographical memory, and confabulation | title = Autobiographical memory | editor = Rubin D | pages = [https://archive.org/details/autobiographical0000unse_g9s2/page/225 225–252] | date = 1986 | doi = 10.1017/CBO9780511558313.020 | isbn = 9780521303224 | chapter-url = https://archive.org/details/autobiographical0000unse_g9s2/page/225 }}</ref>
#	Levine and colleagues designed the Autobiographical Interview to distinguish between episodic and semantic components of episodic memories based on participants' verbal descriptions.<ref name=pmid12507363>{{cite journal |vauthors=Levine B, Svoboda E, Hay JF, Winocur G, Moscovitch M | title = Aging and autobiographical memory: Dissociating episodic from semantic retrieval | journal = Psychology and Aging | volume = 17 | issue = 4 | pages = 677–89 | date = Dec 2002 | pmid = 12507363 | doi=10.1037/0882-7974.17.4.677}}</ref>

===Episodic foresight===
Miloyan and McFarlane performed a systematic review of episodic foresight measurement instruments used in human adults and found that most of these measures were adapted from measures of episodic memory.<ref name=pmid30274668>{{cite journal |vauthors=Miloyan B, McFarlane KA | title = The measurement of episodic foresight: A systematic review of assessment instruments | journal = Cortex | volume = 117 | pages = 351–370 | pmid = 30274668| year = 2019 | doi = 10.1016/j.cortex.2018.08.018 | s2cid = 52899584 }}</ref>
#The measure by Dritschel et al.<ref name=pmid1565011/> based on the [[Controlled Oral Word Association Test]] was adapted by MacLeod and colleagues to assess episodic foresight.<ref name=pmid16368029>{{cite journal |vauthors=MacLeod AK, Tata P, Tyrer P, Schmidt U, Davidson K, Thompson S | title = Hopelessness and positive and negative future thinking in parasuicide | journal = British Journal of Clinical Psychology | volume = 44 | issue = 4 | pages = 495–504 | date = Nov 2005 | pmid = 16368029 | doi=10.1348/014466505x35704}}</ref>
#Williams et al. adapted the 4-point scale from Baddeley and Wilson to assess episodic foresight.<ref name=pmid8822164>{{cite journal |vauthors=Williams JM, Ellis NC, Tyers C, Healy H, Rose G, MacLeod AK | title = The specificity of autobiographical memory and imageability of the future | journal = Memory and Cognition | volume = 24 | issue = 1 | pages = 116–25 | date = Jan 1996 | pmid = 8822164 | doi=10.3758/bf03197278| doi-access = free }}</ref>
#The Autobiographical Interview by Levine and colleagues which was designed to distinguish the episodic and semantic components of episodic memories was adapted by Addis and colleagues to measure episodic foresight.<ref name=pmid18181789>{{cite journal |vauthors=Addis DR, Wong AT, Schacter DL | title = Age-related changes in the episodic simulation of future events | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 19 | issue = 1 | pages = 33–41 | date = Jan 2008 | pmid = 18181789 | doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02043.x| s2cid = 205573448 }}</ref>

The authors of the systematic review noted that a limitation of all such episodic foresight measures is that they do not compare people's simulation of future events to objective preparatory behaviors or to the actual occurrence of future events.<ref name=pmid30274668/> Thus, none of the available measures verify the accuracy or relevance of people's imaginings. This is in contrast to studies of episodic foresight in children and animals that require participants to demonstrate episodic foresight with behaviors to compensate for their lack of verbal ability.

== See also ==

* [[Prospection]]
* [[Time perception]]
